The real secret to the principle of liking is to like the one you're with. For example, in nearly every sales seminar I've ever taken, we were told that the number one rule of sales is to first get your customer to like you. Now, this approach isn't wrong, it's just not the number one rule. It's the number two rule. The number one rule is to first come to like your customer. As we've already emphasized, this is the case because when they see that we genuinely like them, they'll feel safer with us. And provided that we do like them, they'll be right. So that means we have to do our homework to find the things we truly have in common with them, or can sincerely compliment them about, or can genuinely cooperate with them on. Those things, if real, will not only cause them to like us, they'll cause us to like them, which is what they're really looking for in a business partner, not just someone they like, but more importantly, someone who likes them. Finally, I think there's a kind of irony associated with these activators of liking. The activators of similarity, praise, and cooperation are so obvious, so simple, that a lot of times we forget to use them. We fumble them away because our heads are elsewhere, developing some detail or focusing on some minor feature of whatever it is we are presenting to a customer. And in the process, we forget to pay attention to the potent fundamentals of influence, like establishing liking through the activators we've discussed. Remember the study in which people didn't see a gorilla that was right there in the situation because they were focused on some trivial thing? Well, each of the three activators of liking is a persuasion gorilla, and we'd be regrettable bunglers of the influence process if we fail to harness this gorilla-like force when it's right there waiting for us to channel it into benefit for all concerned.
